segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with an upbeat, driving background music as Gordon Ramsay, dressed in a black t-shirt, stands in a modern, well-lit kitchen, posing the question, "What happened when Spider-Man and an Iron Man come together?" Tom Holland, wearing a beige button-up shirt and a maroon apron with 'BERO' printed on it, animatedly responds, "We make sandwiches." Gordon enthusiastically agrees, then humorously instructs, "Please, no backflips," to which Tom replies, "No backflips," and recounts a past incident where he broke his nose doing a backflip in Colombia. The energetic music swells as a title card appears, displaying 'SCRAMBLED with Gordon Ramsay', sponsored by HEXCLAD, with a stylized egg yolk splash. Gordon formally welcomes viewers to "Scrambled in the stunning Gordon Ramsay Academy," introducing Tom Holland as "an incredible actor, an amazing young man, the founder of BERO." Tom thanks Gordon, and they engage in a friendly conversation, with Tom detailing his busy schedule, including "prepping for Spider-Man 4" and "working on BERO." Gordon then asks if Tom ever imagined becoming a superhero, to which Tom emphatically replies, "No, not at all," explaining he was always a "good athlete" but on the "B team." Gordon playfully challenges this, but Tom maintains his stance, mentioning his brothers were on the A-teams. The scene cuts between close-ups of both men as they interact, with Tom looking engaged and occasionally glancing at Gordon, while Gordon gestures toward a counter filled with ingredients, including onions, limes, and several prominently displayed gold 'BERO' cans. The setting briefly transitions to a professional kitchen with industrial equipment and then to a dimly lit restaurant, before returning to the bright, modern kitchen where Gordon announces they will make an "amazing sandwich" with an "incredible marinade," as Tom holds a lime, ready to begin the collaborative cooking demonstration.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens in a bright, modern stainless steel kitchen where Gordon Ramsay, a man with spiky blonde hair wearing a black t-shirt, animatedly speaks while holding a gold-colored BERO can. He discusses starting with buttermilk and unique ingredients to tenderize chicken, then transitions to actively chopping cabbage on a wooden board. Tom Holland, with curly brown hair, wearing a beige button-up shirt and a maroon apron with 'BERO' printed on it, appears beside him, listening intently and responding with expressive gestures. Holland explains their goal was to create a non-alcoholic drink, synonymous with sobriety, that removes the typical 'what non-alcoholic drinks do you have?' question at bars, even playfully mentioning 'BERO Hero Spider-Man.' As Ramsay coats chicken pieces in flour and places them on a tray, he interjects, admitting he wasn't a massive fan of non-alcoholic drinks previously but BERO has 'definitely, definitely changed that perception' because 'it does taste like the real thing.' Holland then asks, 'How hard was that?' before elaborating on BERO's 'very different process,' brewed in a unique way by their brew master, Grant Wood, using a special yeast they believe they're the only company to use. The two continue their collaborative cooking, with Holland chopping cabbage, as he concludes by stating he tried 'every single non-alcoholic beer under the sun' during BERO's development, all within the clean, professional kitchen environment where several BERO cans are prominently displayed.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens in a modern, well-lit stainless steel kitchen where Gordon Ramsay, a man with spiky blonde hair and a black t-shirt, is intensely focused, coating chicken pieces in flour on a metal tray. Beside him, Tom Holland, with curly brown hair and wearing a maroon 'BERO' apron, concentrates on chopping cabbage. Gordon vividly describes a "long process" where for a long time, the product "tasted like sh*t" and was "minging," to which Tom agrees, "it's a process, right?" Gordon, gesturing expressively, recounts how "one day he just turned it on" and "turned in three different skewers, which we all fell in love with," praising "Grant Woody" as "fantastic." Tom affirms, "And so should we, it's delicious," with Gordon responding, "Thank you." Throughout, several gold-colored BERO cans are prominently displayed on the counter, with Gordon handling one at a point, as the camera alternates between close-ups of their faces and wider shots, capturing their energetic and collaborative culinary efforts amidst the sounds of their engaging dialogue and subtle kitchen activity.

Tom, speaking with a British accent, responds enthusiastically about his acting career, stating, "Oh, dude, I mean, every young British actor, it's the pinnacle of... working in our industry... The camera pans to show the kitchen setup: a tray of fried chicken, buns, lime, shredded cabbage, and several gold BERO cans arranged on the counter, one of which Gordon picks up. Tom, focused on preparation, chops cabbage and cracks an egg into a large metal bowl. He then asks Gordon, "And how many of these should I put in?" Gordon, giving instructions, explains they'll "dress it lightly," emphasizing "Nothing worse with a sort of heavy cloy... We want it nice and light." Tom confirms, "Two tablespoons," as Gordon details adding "fresh lime juice, a little bit of lime zest... Salt, pepper, a little touch of hot sauce," before they "start putting this beautiful bad boy together." Gordon then passionately explains the benefits of an ingredient, likely from the BERO can, stating, "whilst this is nice and sort of fruity... the tenderization it does in the chicken is off the charts." Tom, surprised, asks, "Really?" Gordon confirms, "Yeah, it really, really good." Tom then poses an insightful question, "Do you find that the lack of presence of alcohol makes a difference in that process?" Gordon elaborates on the "flavor profile" and "lack of alcohol." segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens in a modern stainless steel kitchen where Gordon Ramsay, in a black t-shirt, is actively assembling a dish, layering shredded cabbage onto buns. Tom's voice is heard discussing how friends live close by, humorously adding that the only reason they visit his house is for the air conditioning and his "nice and cool" kitchen. As Gordon continues to layer creamy sauce and fried chicken onto the buns, the camera alternates between close-ups of his hands and wider shots of the two men. Gordon, with a distinct, direct voice, then asks Tom if he managed to "get your head around that story of Billy Elliot," calling it "quite groundbreaking." Tom responds, confirming he saw the musical, and Gordon interjects, calling it "an amazing piece of work." Tom continues, describing how "there wasn't a dry eye in the theater." Gordon then asks if Tom has "been to Newcastle," to which Tom replies, acknowledging the "devastating" nature of the setting. Gordon further probes about the impact of playing such a part, while Tom, gesturing expressively, reflects on being "so caught up in the idea of being young and on the West End" and performing for thousands. He then shares his grown-up perspective, appreciating the musical as being "for the everyday man," describing a "dad crying while he's singing to his son about the hardships of going back to work when he's on strike." Throughout this conversation, a can labeled 'BERO' is visible on the counter, and Gordon picks one up. The dynamic and collaborative atmosphere persists as Gordon places the final piece of fried chicken, completing the dish.
